---
title: "Patchwork"
format: html
editor: source
---

```{r}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, patchwork, ggtext, here)
theme_set(theme_minimal())
```

```{r}
dt = read_csv("company_financials_csv.zip",
              show_col_types = FALSE)
```

Saving the plot to the disk and then reading it back. This will reproduce the actual visualization

```{r}
p1 = dt |> 
  filter(tic == "AAPL") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = datadate, y = oiadpq)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-1000, 42000))

ggsave(here::here("Plots", "plot1.png"), plot = p1,
       dpi = 300, width = 7, height = 5, bg = "white")
```


```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Plots", "plot1.png"))
```

```{r}
p2 = dt |> 
  filter(tic == "MSFT") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = datadate, y = oiadpq)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-1000, 42000))

p2
```

Use Patchwork to position p1 and p2 side by side.


```{r}
p1 + p2
```

One on top of other

```{r}
p1/p2
```

You can achieve this using `facet_wrap()` easily. Then why to build a new package?

Here is why: Using patchwork, you can combine any types of plots. They need not be using the same geom.

```{r}
p3 = dt |> 
  filter(tic == "AAPL" & fyearq >= 2005) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = datadate, y = oiadpq)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = as.factor(fqtr))) +
  labs(fill = "Quarter")

p3
```

```{r}
p1 + p3
```
```{r}
ggsave(here::here("Plots", "plot1_3.png"), plot = p1 + p3,
       dpi = 300, width = 9, height = 4, bg = "white")
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Plots", "plot1_3.png"))
```

Plot annotations

```{r}
p1 + p3 +
  plot_annotation(title = "Apple\'s **quarterly profits** over the years",
                  theme = theme(plot.title = element_textbox_simple(fill = "#acc0cf",
                                                                    padding = margin(10,10,10,10))))
```

```{r}
p1 + p2/p3
```

```{r}
(p1 + p2)/p3
```

```{r}
p3/(p1 + p2)
```
